Lecture_06

#Lecture 06

library("tidyverse")
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ ggplot2   4.0.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ purrr     1.2.1     
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library("palmerpenguins")
## 
## Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'
## 
## The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':
## 
##     penguins, penguins_raw
penguins <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, sex)

#Q1

# Which island has the most penguins? Answer: Biscoe has the most penguins
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = island)) +
  geom_bar() 

# When adding theme_minimal(), what changes visually? Answer: The shaded background disappears 
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = island)) +
  geom_bar() +
  theme_minimal()

#Q2

# What is the general shape of the distribution (normal? bimodal?) Answer: The general shape is normal.
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram()
## `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.

# How does bin number change what you see? Answer: The higher the binwidth number, the thicker the bars of the histogram become. 
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10)

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 30)

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 60)

# How does the distribution differ between species? Answer: The body mass ranges are different for each species, Adelie = 2500-4700, Chinstrap =3500-4700 with outliers, Gentoo 4500-
# Do they all have the same approximate mean? Answer: No, Adelie= 3400-3600, Chinstrap = 3600, Gentoo= 5500
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 60) +
  facet_wrap(~ species)

#Q3

# Which species has the longest flippers (typical value)? Answer: Gentoo has the longest flippers
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_boxplot()

# Do males and females differ? Answer: In general, yes, males tend to be larger in body mass
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_boxplot()

# Does adding fill = species to the aesthetics (aes()) help readability? Answer: Yes, instead of 2 boxplots, there are 6 that are color coded and separated by species and sex. There are also indicated outliers.
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g, fill= species)) +
  geom_boxplot()

#Q4

# Is the relationship positive, negative, or unclear? Answer: The relationship is positive or more likely unclear. The points go in one general direction from the bottom to top, but still have many points that remain below the "positive" direction.
ggplot(penguins, aes(x=bill_length_mm, y= body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point()

# Color the plot by species (color = species in aes()). Does that change your interpretation? It does, the relationship is positive for each species(Chinstrap moves more towards unclear), but the distinction helps make it clearer to understand.
ggplot(penguins, aes(x=bill_length_mm, y= body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point()

# Does the line fit the data well after adding geom_smooth(method = "lm")? Answer: yes, there are three lines that fit each species and describe a positive relationship for each species (except for Chinstrap which still has many points far from the line)
ggplot(penguins, aes(x=bill_length_mm, y= body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

#Q5
# Which species shows the strongest relationship? Answer: Gentoo has the strongest relationship
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~species)

# What patterns appear? Answer: Females have relatively smaller body masses and flipper length compared to males across all species. The positive relationship is less visible.
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_grid(sex~species)

#Q6

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = island, y = bill_depth_mm,  color = species)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "pink") +
  geom_jitter (width = 0.4, alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~sex) +
  labs(x= "Island", y= "Bill Depth (mm)") +
  theme_light(base_size = 12) + 
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set3")

#Q7 # The most fun/interesting part was Q6 and choosing colors from the sets and seeing what colors existed/registered. The most challenging part was remembering which function # did what, specifically for the color and theme and figuring out the purpose of x any y in labs vs setting it in aes() . Also one of the plot types include geom_scatter(), which appears to be nonexistent, which was confusing.

#Lecture 07

penguins_messy <- read.csv("penguins_messy.csv")

#Q1

# This command detects if the string "adelie" is present within the datasets species category. Species include "adelie", "gentoo" and "chinstrap", which returns TRUE if "adelie" is present and FALSE if anything else.
str_detect(penguins_messy$species_messy, "adelie") 
##   [1]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [13]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [25]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [37]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [49]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [61]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [73]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [85]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [97]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [109]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [121]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [133]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [145]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [157] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [169] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [181] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [193] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [205] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [217] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [229] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [241] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [253] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [265] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [277] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [289] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [301] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [313] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [325] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [337] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
# This command replaces question marks(?) with nothing, essentially deleting the question marks present in the M/f for the sex category of the dataset.
str_replace_all(penguins_messy$sex_messy, "\\?", "")
##   [1] "M" "f" "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  NA  NA  NA  "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M"
##  [19] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [37] "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [55] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [73] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f"
##  [91] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"

## [163] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  "M"
## [181] "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f"
## [199] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"

## [271] "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"

## [343] "M" "f"
# This command cuts and collapses repeated whitespace within the island dataset names.
str_squish(penguins_messy$island_messy)  
##   [1] "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen"

#Q2

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(valid_id = str_detect(penguins_messy$sample_id, "PAL-[0-9]{3}"))
penguins_messy

# Number of rows: 316 
penguins_messy %>% 
  pull(valid_id) %>% 
  sum
## [1] 316

#Q3

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  filter(valid_id == TRUE) 
penguins_messy

#Q4

penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(file_date = str_extract(file_name, "^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}"),file_id = str_extract(file_name, "PAL-[0-9]{3}"))%>%
head(10)
##    species_messy island_messy sex_messy sample_id
## 1         adelie  Torgersen\t         M   PAL-535
## 2         adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-442
## 3         adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-668
## 4         adelie  Torgersen\t      <NA>   PAL-920
## 5         adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-223
## 6         adelie  Torgersen\t         M   PAL-899
## 7         adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-268
## 8         adelie  Torgersen\t         M   PAL-139
## 9         adelie  Torgersen\t      <NA>   PAL-847
## 10        adelie  Torgersen\t      <NA>   PAL-238
##                                    file_name
## 1  2024-01-14_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-535.csv
## 2  2024-01-17_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-442.csv

## 9  2024-01-14_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-847.csv
## 10 2024-01-24_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-238.csv
##                                                     notes valid_id  file_date
## 1  id=PAL-535; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=39.1mm     TRUE 2024-01-14
## 2  id=PAL-442; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=39.5mm     TRUE 2024-01-17
## 3  id=PAL-668; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=40.3mm     TRUE 2024-01-08

## 9  id=PAL-847; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=34.1mm     TRUE 2024-01-14
## 10   id=PAL-238; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=42mm     TRUE 2024-01-24
##    file_id
## 1  PAL-535
## 2  PAL-442
## 3  PAL-668
## 4  PAL-920
## 5  PAL-223
## 6  PAL-899
## 7  PAL-268
## 8  PAL-139
## 9  PAL-847
## 10 PAL-238

#Q5

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(island = str_squish(island_messy))
unique(penguins_messy$island)
## [1] "Torgersen" "BIS-COE"   "Dream"

#Q6

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(sex = str_to_upper(str_replace_all(sex_messy, "\\?", ""), ))
unique(penguins_messy$sex)
## [1] "M" "F" NA

#Q7

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(bill_mm = str_replace_all(notes, "(.*bill=)|mm", ""))
penguins_messy

## 259    F    46.1
## 260    M    51.3
## 261    F      46
## 262    M    51.3
## 263    M    51.7
## 264    F      47
## 265    M      52
## 266    F    45.9
## 267    M    50.5
## 268    M    50.3
## 269    F      58
## 270    F    46.4
## 271    M    49.2
## 272    F    42.4
## 273    M    48.5
## 274    F    43.2
## 275    M    50.6
## 276    F    46.7
## 277    M      52
## 278    F    50.5
## 279    M    49.5
## 280    F    46.4
## 281    M    52.8
## 282    F    40.9
## 283    M    54.2
## 284    F    42.5
## 285    M      51
## 286    M    49.7
## 287    F    47.5
## 288    F    47.6
## 289    M      52
## 290    F    46.9
## 291    M    53.5
## 292    M      49
## 293    M    50.9
## 294    F    45.5
## 295    F    50.9
## 296    M    50.8
## 297    F    50.1
## 298    M      49
## 299    M    51.5
## 300    F    49.8
## 301    F    48.1
## 302    M    51.4
## 303    F    45.7
## 304    M    50.7
## 305    F    42.5
## 306    M    52.2
## 307    M    50.2
## 308    F    45.6
## 309    M    51.9
## 310    F    46.8
## 311    F    45.7
## 312    M    55.8
## 313    F    43.5
## 314    M    49.6
## 315    M    50.8
## 316    F    50.2

#Q8 # The most fun part about this exercise was figuring out the str_to_upper(), but it was also the most difficult, as it took too long to get both of the sexes to be capitalized for some reason, and I finally found that nesting it was the only way it would work. I think I have a general idea of how regex are used, but it is difficult to recall the symbols.

#Lecture 08

#Q1

penguins <- palmerpenguins::penguins

penguins_clean<- penguins %>% 
  drop_na(bill_length_mm, sex)

# There are 344 rows in penguins
nrow(penguins)
## [1] 344
# There are 333 rows in penguins_clean
nrow(penguins_clean)
## [1] 333

#Q2

penguins_dup <- penguins_clean %>%
  bind_rows(slice_sample(penguins_clean, n = 30, replace = TRUE))
nrow(penguins_dup)
## [1] 363
nrow(distinct(penguins_dup))
## [1] 333
# Duplicates can mess up summary statistics and plots by overrepresenting data and inflating sample size, in addition to altering mean values.

#Q3

# How many columns are in the resulting data frame? Answer: 8
penguins_clean %>%
  select(species, island, sex, where(is.numeric))
## # A tibble: 333 × 8
##    species island    sex    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##    <fct>   <fct>     <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torgersen male             39.1          18.7               181
##  2 Adelie  Torgersen female           39.5          17.4               186
##  3 Adelie  Torgersen female           40.3          18                 195
##  4 Adelie  Torgersen female           36.7          19.3               193
##  5 Adelie  Torgersen male             39.3          20.6               190
##  6 Adelie  Torgersen female           38.9          17.8               181
##  7 Adelie  Torgersen male             39.2          19.6               195
##  8 Adelie  Torgersen female           41.1          17.6               182
##  9 Adelie  Torgersen male             38.6          21.2               191
## 10 Adelie  Torgersen male             34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows
## # ℹ 2 more variables: body_mass_g <int>, year <int>
ncol(penguins_clean)
## [1] 8

#Q4

penguins_clean %>%
  select(starts_with("bill")) %>%
  select(ends_with("_mm")) %>%
  select(matches("bill|flipper"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 2
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>
##  1           39.1          18.7
##  2           39.5          17.4
##  3           40.3          18  
##  4           36.7          19.3
##  5           39.3          20.6
##  6           38.9          17.8
##  7           39.2          19.6
##  8           41.1          17.6
##  9           38.6          21.2
## 10           34.6          21.1
## # ℹ 323 more rows

#Q5

# Why is across(where(is.numeric), …) better than typing out every numeric column name by hand? Answer: The across function is faster and easier. Its beneficial for summarizing groups by specific data types.
penguins_clean %>%
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarize(across(where(is.numeric), mean))
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   species   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g  year
##   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 Adelie              38.8          18.3              190.       3706. 2008.
## 2 Chinstrap           48.8          18.4              196.       3733. 2008.
## 3 Gentoo              47.6          15.0              217.       5092. 2008.

#Q6

penguins_clean %>%
  mutate(bill_length = 
  case_when(
    bill_length_mm < 40 ~ "short",
    bill_length_mm < 50 ~ "medium",
    bill_length_mm >= 50 ~ "long")         
  ) %>%
  select(bill_length_mm, bill_length)
## # A tibble: 333 × 2
##    bill_length_mm bill_length
##             <dbl> <chr>      
##  1           39.1 short      
##  2           39.5 short      
##  3           40.3 medium     
##  4           36.7 short      
##  5           39.3 short      
##  6           38.9 short      
##  7           39.2 short      
##  8           41.1 medium     
##  9           38.6 short      
## 10           34.6 short      
## # ℹ 323 more rows

#Q7

instrument_meta <- tibble(
  instrument_id = c("caliper_A", "caliper_B", "caliper_C"),
  calibration_mm = c(0.2, 0.5, 1.0),
  manufacturer = c("Mitutoyo", "Fowler", "Generic")
)
penguins_instrumented <- penguins_clean %>%
  drop_na(bill_length_mm) %>%
  mutate(instrument_id =
  case_when(
      year == 2007 ~ "caliper_A",
      year == 2008 ~ "caliper_B",
      year == 2009 ~ "caliper_C")
  ) %>%
  select(year, instrument_id) %>% distinct
left_join(penguins_instrumented, instrument_meta, by = "instrument_id")
## # A tibble: 3 × 4
##    year instrument_id calibration_mm manufacturer
##   <int> <chr>                  <dbl> <chr>       
## 1  2007 caliper_A                0.2 Mitutoyo    
## 2  2008 caliper_B                0.5 Fowler      
## 3  2009 caliper_C                1   Generic
# What is the number of columns in penguins_instrumented and penguins_clean?: Answer: There are 4 columns

#Q8 # The most interesting part of the exercise was adding columns and seeing the correct result(hopefully) and the most difficult was number 7, which included adding everything we learned for lecture 08. The left_join() was the most difficult to work out, but looking a the notes, I noticed what was missing was the distinct in the line of code above it. I can see the code from this lecture will be beneficial to data analyses in the future because a lot of it simplifiies both the code writing process and the interpretation of it.
